label.title: Spectral Mean Normalization Results Following Avendano Method
label.summary: Implemented a spectral mean subtraction technique based on Avendano's approach using 2-second FFT frames stepped by 0.5 seconds, covering 6 seconds of speech (current frame plus 4 past and 4 future frames). key_points: Method uses 2-second FFT analysis frames stepped by 0.5 seconds (quarter-length step)
Each processing window combines current frame + 4 past frames + 4 future frames = 6 seconds of speech total

All means are calculated first, then subtraction is applied afterward (not done frame-by-frame sequentially)
Tested with HDK Aurora setup trained on clean TIDIGITS

Model was trained on clean speech only, likely explaining the poor baseline performance

label.title: Spectral Cues for Speech vs Nonspeech Detection
label.summary: This memo discusses why pure energy-based detection fails to distinguish low-energy speech from nonspeech noise, proposing that spectral slope characteristics can help differentiate them. Unvoiced speech sounds (like the start of 'S') tend to have high-frequency/high-pass characteristics, while background noise and rumble tend to be low-frequency/low-pass, and this approach echoes techniques from Rabiner and Schafer's older work.
key_points: Pure energy-based analysis cannot reliably distinguish low-energy speech from nonspeech noise.
Most nonspeech noises exhibit a low-pass (downward sloping) spectral characteristic.
Low-energy unvoiced speech components (e.g., onset of an 'S' sound) tend to have a high-pass (upward sloping) spectral characteristic.
Background rumble and noise are predominantly low-frequency.
Spectral slope alone is insufficient, but combined with energy and timing information it becomes a useful detection method.
This combined approach is similar to techniques described in Rabiner and Schafer's work from roughly 25 years prior.

label.title: Discourse Model Architecture and Query Depth Design
label.summary: This memo describes how a dialogue system parses user intention and passes it to a discourse history module that maintains memory of the entire conversation, including anaphora resolution and gesture/screen coordination. key_points: The parser first identifies general intention (e.g., wanting to go somewhere) without fully differentiating modes
The discourse history module acts as the system's overall memory, tracking who said what and what was presented
This module handles anaphora resolution and fills in omitted structural information across dialogue turns
Information persists across turns unless explicitly rejected by the user (e.g., changing plans)
The discourse model also coordinates gesture/screen interactions by storing what was displayed and where
The module has a clear query interface, e.g., checking whether admission fees were discussed in prior turns
Not every module retains memory of the full conversation, making the discourse model essential
questions: How deep should the system search back into discourse history when querying past turns?
What is a reasonable limit for historical search given the research context and typically concise dialogues?

The key difference is selective updating (stationary-only) versus blanket averaging.
